schools.summary: Matthew Henry turns the Jebusite threshing-floor into prophecy — a presage of the gospel temple among the Gentiles — and grounds David's urgent preparation in his approaching death.
Ellicott resumes the narrative of 1 Chron. 21:28, names the site 'a house of God' after Gen. 28:17, and reads the whole census story as preserved only to show how the Temple site was fixed.
Clarke makes this the temple and altar until one is built, superseding Gibeon, and has David buy adjoining ground; Wesley grounds David's word in the direction of God's Spirit.
Gill notes only that David spoke within himself or to some principal persons about him.
Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott identify the gerim as the subjugated Canaanite remnant liable to forced service, numbered by census at 153,600, and set to hew squared stones.
Gill takes the strangers for proselytes, spares the freeborn Israelite the servile work, and finds a mystery — the Gentiles' share in building the spiritual house.
Clarke calls them proselytes keeping the seven Noahic precepts, employed in the more laborious parts of the work.
Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott expound the joinery — pivots, cramps, folding-doors — and the bronze of ancient gates, taking 'without weight' as a natural hyperbole the treasurers would have tallied.
Gill keeps to the plain sense: iron for the nails and hinges of the doors and gates.

Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott set the cedar in its Phoenician trade — bartered for Israel's grain and wine — and read 'cedars without number' as rhetorical, anticipating Solomon's later treaty with Hiram.
Gill fixes on the finished use of the cedar: boards and planks for the ceiling, wainscotting, and flooring.

Keil and Delitzsch hold Solomon not quite twenty and hold na'ar to the boyish age; Ellicott presses the word's vagueness (babe to man in prime), reads David's resolve as self-encouragement, and defends the scene against the charge of fiction.
Gill reports Jarchi's guess of about twelve but inclines himself to about twenty.
Wesley gives the reason the house could not be scanted: it was for the honour of the great God and a type of Christ, in whom all fulness dwells.

debates.question: How old was Solomon when David charged him?

outliers.note: Defends the whole episode of David's preparations against Gramberg's charge that it is a fiction of the Chronicler's, merely because Samuel and Kings are silent on it.

schools.summary: Matthew Henry explains why David could not build — the blood of his wars, not of Uriah — and gathers the reasons pressed on Solomon: God's designation, his rest, and the promised throne.
Keil and Delitzsch draw out the commissioning and the fitting address 'my son'; Ellicott judges the whole dying speech an ideal composition of the Chronicler's, stressing 'the God of Israel.'
Gill notes only that Solomon was brought before him.
Clarke takes 'young and tender' to mean without complete knowledge and experience, so David must prepare all he could.

debates.question: To what 'much blood' does the bar refer — the blood of Uriah or the blood of David's wars?

sides.position: the blood of Uriah, a moral reproach
the blood shed in his many wars, though shed justly

themes: blood of Uriah or of war

schools.summary: Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott defend the reading 'my son' over 'his son,' gloss 'it was in my mind' as a late Chronicler's idiom, and read the verse as looking back to the Nathan promise of 1 Chron. 17.
Gill marks only the setting, that David spoke when Solomon was brought into his presence.

themes: it was in my mind to build

schools.summary: Clarke reads the bar as a ritual unfitness — soldiers and bloodshed ill comport with divine offices (with a Virgilian illustration); Wesley refuses this, making it typological, since David's wars were lawful but the temple prefigures the peaceful church.

Ellicott complicates both: the oracle only partly matches Nathan's word, 1 Kings 5:3 pleads want of leisure, and 'torrents of blood before me' may echo Gen. 9:5 and the prophets, or David's own conscience.
Gill takes 'the word of the LORD' for the word of prophecy, delivered by Nathan per the Targum.

debates.question: Why does the shedding of blood bar David from building the temple?

sides.position: a ritual and moral unfitness — soldiers and bloodshed ill comport with divine offices
a typological fitness — the temple, type of the church, must be built by the Prince of Peace, though David's wars were lawful
not warfare as such but the want of a peaceful reign's leisure, or David's own conscience construing the oracle

schools.summary: Ellicott sets 'man of rest' against 'man of war,' traces the name Shelomoh across its cognate languages, and recalls that the child's God-given name was Jedidiah, though posterity remembered him as the Peaceful.
Clarke draws the pun: Shelomoh from shalam, 'he was peaceable,' so God promises shalom in his days.
Gill notes the promise was given before Solomon's birth, a word over an unborn child.
Ellicott reads the verse as repeating the Nathan oracle of 1 Chron. 17 — the house, the divine sonship, and the throne established for ever bound in one promise.
Gill glosses 'a house for my name' as built for the worship, honour, and glory of God.

themes: build a house for the Name

schools.summary: Ellicott reads 'the Lord be with thee' as a wish, notes it as the origin of the liturgical formula, and binds 'as he hath said of thee' to the divine promise already given.
Gill hears prayer and prediction together — a prayer of faith — citing the Targum's 'may the Word of the Lord be thine help.'

themes: the origin of a liturgical formula

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch make obedience the hinge — the promised result hangs on keeping the law; Ellicott renders 'at least may the Lord give' and 'appoint thee over Israel,' the wisdom sought for the sake of law-keeping.
Gill takes the wisdom as asked both for the great affair and for the government of the people.

Ellicott makes obedience the indispensable condition, hears Deuteronomy throughout ('be stout and staunch'), reads 'dismayed' as 'broken' answering the timid youth, and finds the promise unbroken in the greater Son.
Gill notes the same charge was given elsewhere, showing it was spoken a little before David's death.
Clarke computes the talents literally (some 861 million pounds) as a real hoard from David's conquests; Wesley defends the figures as possible, most spent on workmen and laid up in the treasures.

Keil and Delitzsch read 'by my painful labour' (not 'in my poverty') and reckon the value; Ellicott calls the figures incredibly large, deliberate Oriental hyperbole whose very symmetry argues against textual corruption.
Matthew Henry calls the sum incredible — most allow an error in the copy, or take 'talent' for an ingot; he inclines to a definite number put for an indefinite.
Gill reads 'in my trouble' of the wars and rebellions that dogged David's reign.

debates.question: How are the immense quantities of gold and silver to be construed?

sides.position: literal, real treasure amassed from David's conquests
a textual error, or 'talent' meaning an ingot, or a definite number put for an indefinite
deliberate Oriental hyperbole, not to be pressed literally

themes: a hundred thousand talents of gold

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch expand 'hewers' into masons and carpenters and 'cunning men' into master-craftsmen of thorough skill, the very workmen Solomon would acknowledge to Hiram.
Gill notes the strangers gathered at David's order, standing ready in abundance, skilled in every manner of work.
Keil and Delitzsch group the metals and render 'the gold cannot be numbered — arise and be doing'; Ellicott notes the summons 'arise and be doing' recurs at Ezra 10:4.
Wesley: the sense of God's presence must not slacken but quicken endeavour — because he is with us, we must rise and be doing; 'work out your salvation, and God will work in you.'
Gill takes the metals to name the cunning artificers, some working in gold and silver, others in brass and iron.

themes: no number, arise and be doing

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: those on the throne need those about it; the princes owe zeal in gratitude for victory and rest, must seek God with heart and soul, and every hidden labour is a stone toward the temple.
Ellicott notes 'saying' is absent from the Hebrew, an omission paralleled elsewhere in Chronicles.
Gill identifies the princes as David's courtiers and nobles.

themes: seek God with heart and soul
every labour a stone toward the temple
the Prince of Peace the Finisher

schools.summary: Keil, Delitzsch, and Ellicott expound the rest and the subdued land, identify the 'inhabitants' as the conquered Canaanites, and anchor the conquest in David's wars of 1 Chron. 18-20.
Clarke cites the Targum ('the Word of the Lord your assistant') and notes David handed Solomon every advantage.
Gill reads the divine presence concretely, as God's blessing them with wealth and riches.

outliers.note: Turns aside from the verse to lament Solomon's decline — the unlawful marriages and heathen wives that turned his heart — so that 'the once wise and holy Solomon died a fool and an idolater.'

themes: Is not the Lord with you?
the Canaanites given into David's hand
the ruin foreseen at the outset

schools.summary: Ellicott notes 'to seek unto the Lord,' makes building the sanctuary the appointed way of seeking his favour, insists it not be done 'without cost,' and names the end — bringing up the ark and holy vessels.
Gill reads the seeking as the heart's whole engagement for God's honour and glory, sincerely and to the utmost.

themes: set your heart to seek the Lord
building as the way of seeking
